$schema: This is descriptive research evidence, not normative doctrine
note: Extracted theoretical frameworks and recurring ideas from cinema color research. These are descriptive concepts, not prescriptive rules.

frameworks.name: Color Consciousness / Writing with Light
frameworks.description: Color as a psychological and philosophical language rather than decorative element. Color wavelengths physically alter the viewer's metabolism and consciousness.
frameworks.application_context: Used to justify operatic, symbolic color choices that prioritize psychological impact over naturalism

frameworks.name: Available Energy / Jazz of Available Light
frameworks.description: Color is a reaction to the environment, not an imposition upon it. Improvisational approach that allows location and available light to dictate palette.
frameworks.application_context: Validates preservation of 'unflattering' or 'dirty' colors as textural authenticity

frameworks.name: Justified Stylization / Doctrine of Invisibility
frameworks.description: Look must never distract from narrative. Even extreme stylization must be grounded in diegetic source and narrative logic.
frameworks.application_context: Counter-argument to aggressive digital grading. Guides differentiation between naturalistic stylization and digital graphicness

sources.description: Deakins on Blade Runner 2049 and approach to color

frameworks.name: Subtractive Lighting / Politics of Underexposure
frameworks.description: Challenges technical bias of film history calibrated for white skin. Employs subtractive lighting to preserve absorptive quality and texture of black skin.
frameworks.application_context: Essential for understanding cultural context of lighting techniques and moving beyond bright/dark binary

sources.description: Young's discourse on lighting and representation
sources.note: Discusses technical bias and political dimensions of cinematography

frameworks.name: Materiality of Memory / Media-Referential Color
frameworks.description: Audiences respond to 'texture of the medium' as much as content. Prioritizes memory of an era over reality of an era. Texture + Color = Time.
frameworks.application_context: Supports tags that identify film stock emulations (Ektachrome Look, Technicolor Look) as intentional temporal devices

sources.description: Lachman on Carol and approach to period color

frameworks.name: Darkness as Canvas / Alchemist of Shadow
frameworks.description: Manipulation of chemical process to alter 'weight' of image. Lighting for shadows, not highlights.
frameworks.application_context: Defined 1990s aesthetic. Distinction between natural underexposure and chemical underexposure

frameworks.name: Color as Cultural Memory / Artifact of the Era
frameworks.description: Cinema constructs our visual memory of history. We remember the past not as it looked, but as it was photographed. The 'look' of a decade is defined by limitations of its film stock.
frameworks.application_context: Explains why Technicolor = 1940s-50s, Push-Process = 1970s, Ektachrome = 1950s-60s in cultural consciousness

sources.description: Synthesized from multiple sources on film history and perception

frameworks.name: Chromatic Dissonance / Counter-Intuitive Palette
frameworks.description: Using color against its traditional meaning creates narrative power. Warm ≠ Happy, Cool ≠ Sad when inverted intentionally.
frameworks.application_context: Warns against rigid 'Color Dictionaries' (Red = Anger). Supports context-aware tagging system

sources.description: Synthesized from analyses of Moonlight, Midsommar, The Florida Project

frameworks.name: Black & White as Editorial Super-Color
frameworks.description: In color era, B&W is never default; it is violent editorial choice. Functions as 'Super-Color' that alters rules of diegesis.
frameworks.application_context: B&W films must be sub-categorized by texture and intent, not treated as monolithic category

sources.description: Synthesized from analyses of contemporary B&W cinema

frameworks.name: Memory Color Effect (MCE)
frameworks.description: Human memory is 'color diagnostic.' We remember objects not as they are, but as their 'hyper-real' ideal.
frameworks.application_context: Explains why hyper-saturated films (Amélie, La La Land) feel nostalgic - they match our memory of world, not world itself

sources.description: Research on memory color effect and perception

frameworks.name: The Teal & Orange Discourse
frameworks.description: Homogenization of blockbusters into Teal/Orange paradigm (to separate skin tones) treated as 'scourge' by critics. Distinction needed between lazy default and intentional complementary palettes.
frameworks.application_context: Editorial mandate to separate 'Lazy Teal/Orange' (digital default) from 'Intentional Complementary Palettes' (artistic choice)

frameworks.name: The Technicolor Construct
frameworks.description: Three-strip Technicolor process defined mid-century visual imagination. Required immense light, necessitating shadows filled with colored light. Created 'Operatic Saturation' aesthetic.
frameworks.application_context: Films from this era tagged 'Dye-Transfer Aesthetics.' Modern emulations trigger 'Golden Age' nostalgia

sources.description: Analysis of three-strip Technicolor process and Jack Cardiff's work

frameworks.name: The New Hollywood Grit / Push-Process Aesthetics
frameworks.description: 1970s aesthetic driven by Eastman Kodak stocks that could be 'pushed' in development. 'Muddy Realism' - earth tones, grain as texture of authenticity.
frameworks.application_context: Defines 'Retro-Crime' look in modern films like Joker or American Hustle

sources.description: Analysis of 1970s film stocks and push processing

frameworks.name: Neon-Noir / Toxic Neon Aesthetics
frameworks.description: Colors the human eye perceives as 'chemical' or 'toxic' - acid greens, blacklight purples, sodium vapor oranges. Signal altered state of consciousness.
frameworks.application_context: Contemporary aesthetic in Good Time, Spring Breakers. Distinct category from nostalgic neon

sources.description: Analysis of contemporary independent cinema aesthetics

frameworks.name: Bazin's Color Paradox
frameworks.description: Cinema strives for 'total representation of reality,' but early color was seen as 'spectacle' or 'attraction' rather than realistic element. Historical friction between color as distraction vs. immersion.
frameworks.application_context: Informs 'Historical Context' tags - early color was distracting, modern color is immersive

sources.title: The Myth of Total Cinema
sources.author: André Bazin
sources.description: Theoretical framework for understanding color in cinema history
